metadata.notes: Master plant file with structured image roles. First batch had 13 image-ready species from existing hardwired entries; this batch adds sourced and curated image sets for 3 more species (American beech, ramps, basswood). Copyback content merged directly into plants.json from batch10 for 74 matching species on 2026-04-21.

records.notes: Smooth gray-barked tree with alternate toothed leaves and small triangular nuts in prickly husks. This copyback comes from the old beechnut entry and is applied to the consolidated American beech plant record.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food tree.
records.culinary_uses: Nuts are edible and rich when available in good mast years.
records.edibility_detail: Edible nuts, though years of heavy production can vary a lot.
look_alikes: Young ironwood or hornbeam leaves at a distance

records.notes: Small tree with orange fruits that must be truly ripe before they are pleasant. Included as a broad legacy fruit record even though it is more southern than many UP core species.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit tree in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Very ripe fruits are eaten fresh or used in puddings, breads, and preserves when soft enough to lose their harsh astringency.
records.edibility_detail: Unripe fruit is intensely astringent. Wait until fully soft and ripe.

records.notes: Large wet-ground Apiaceae with purple-tinged stems and umbrella flower clusters. Correct identification is non-negotiable.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-heavy edible species.
records.culinary_uses: Young stalks and some parts have old traditional use, but this is a caution-heavy edible because carrot-family confusion can go bad fast.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic medicinal plant.
records.edibility_detail: This is a knowledge plant, not a beginner nibble. Treat toxic-lookalike risk seriously.

records.notes: Spiny shrub with small leaves and elongated tart red berries.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, especially associated with roots and bark in older herbal systems.
records.culinary_uses: Tart berries are used in preserves, sauces, and drying rather than carefree fresh eating.
records.edibility_detail: Edible berries, usually best processed.

records.notes: Tree with alternate heart-shaped leaves, soft texture when young, and fragrant pale flowers later in season.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing tea tree; flowers are more often the medicinally used part.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are mild salad greens, and flowers are commonly used for tea. Tender heart-shaped young leaves are especially good fresh in salads or sandwiches.
records.edibility_detail: Best as young leaves and blossoms; older leaves become less tender.

records.notes: Trailing evergreen shrub of dry sandy places with leathery leaves and red berries.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Well-known traditional urinary herb.
records.culinary_uses: Red berries are edible though mealy; leaves also have traditional tea use.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but the fruit is mealy and usually not considered a prime fresh-eating berry.

records.notes: Mint-family plant with opposite leaves, square stems, and lavender to pink shaggy flower heads.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea herb for colds, mild sore throat, and general aromatic support.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves can be used fresh or dried for tea and aromatic seasoning.

records.notes: Recognize the tree first: papery or peeling bark on many species, fine twig structure, and catkins. The app keeps birch as one tree-level entry while describing the useful parts.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use centers on sap, bark, and leaf tea as mild tonic or urinary-support material.
records.culinary_uses: Sap can be tapped in late winter to spring for fresh drinking or reduction into syrup; inner bark and cambium have historical famine-food use but sap is the main practical foraging use.
records.edibility_detail: Sap is the main beginner-friendly use. Bark/cambium uses are more situational and should not be confused with routine harvesting.

records.notes: Tall tree with dark flaky bark on older trunks, drooping racemes of white flowers, and dark purple-black fruits.
records.short_reason: Added during generic cherry bucket cleanup.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional bark use exists, but the fruit is the main food-side legacy interest.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are used for jelly, syrup, wine, and cooked fruit preparations rather than eaten carelessly by the handful.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe fruit is edible; pits and wilted foliage are not food. Processing is common.

records.notes: Ribes shrub with aromatic foliage and dark fruits borne in clusters.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional berry and leaf use exists, though it is mostly a fruit crop style entry here.
records.culinary_uses: Strongly flavored berries are excellent in jam, syrup, jelly, baking, and juice.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries with a strong currant flavor.

records.notes: Arching bramble with whitish cane bloom and hollow-centered black fruits when picked.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry also used in jam, syrup, desserts, and preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best fully dark and easily removed from the receptacle.

records.notes: Compatibility bucket for multiple true blueberry species in the new app.
records.short_reason: Kept as a compatibility bucket while splitting blueberries into species records.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry group in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Broad blueberry bucket covering fresh eating, baking, syrup, jam, drying, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries; flavor and size vary by species and site.

records.notes: Low woodland dogwood with whorled leaves, white dogwood-like bracts, and clusters of red berries later in season.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Red berries are edible though mild and are more of a trail nibble or mixed-berry addition than a heavy-yield fruit.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional northern use exists, though it is more often treated as a woodland edible than a major medicinal species.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but mild. Best understood as a small northern woodland fruit rather than a major calorie species.

records.notes: Big-leaved biennial with burr-producing flower heads later in life.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional cleansing and tonic herb.
records.culinary_uses: First-year roots are cooked as a root vegetable and young stalks may also be used.
records.edibility_detail: Best in the first year before flowering. Older roots become woody.

records.notes: Tall wetland plant with long flat leaves arising in dense basal fans and distinctive sausage-like flower spikes. Clean water matters a lot.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Some traditional topical use, though mainly an edible wetland staple here.
records.culinary_uses: Shoots, immature flower spikes, pollen, and rhizomes all have food use with proper timing and preparation. Tender inner shoots can be eaten raw or cooked and have a mild cucumber-like to green-corn flavor.
records.edibility_detail: Many edible parts, but harvest timing matters and clean water matters even more.

records.notes: Delicate low weed with opposite small leaves, tiny white starry flowers, and a single line of hairs along the stem.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional cooling and soothing herb.
records.culinary_uses: Tender fresh green used in salads, sandwiches, and light cooked dishes.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and best fresh when tender.

records.notes: Roadside plant with bright blue flowers, stiff branching stems, and a basal rosette in earlier growth.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive bitter and tonic herb, especially focused on the root.
records.culinary_uses: Roots are roasted for a coffee-like flavoring and young leaves can be eaten as bitter greens; flowers are sometimes used decoratively or in small amounts.
records.edibility_detail: Root and young leaves are the main uses. Leaf bitterness is real, and roots are the practical old-app copyback focus.

records.notes: Shrub with white flowers, shiny leaves, and dark berry clusters that can be quite astringent fresh.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Often valued for antioxidant-rich fruit in modern herbal/nutritional discussion.
records.culinary_uses: Dark berries are usually processed into juice, jam, wine, syrup, or mixed-fruit preserves rather than eaten as easy fresh fruit.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but usually better processed than eaten plain.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with hanging flower racemes and dense clusters of dark fruits.
records.short_reason: Species-level chokecherry record kept on the original slug for compatibility.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional bark and fruit use exists, though fruit processing is the practical food use.
records.culinary_uses: Astringent fruits are best cooked into jelly, syrup, wine, or dried preparations.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but usually processed because of puckery astringency.

records.notes: This copyback is based on the old red clover entry and applied to the broader clover record in the new app. Common meadow legume with trifoliate leaves and rounded pink to purple flower heads.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional herb for tea and general tonic use.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers and young leaves are used in teas, salads, and dried food blends.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, though often more useful as tea or adjunct food than a main vegetable.

records.notes: Opposite leaves when young, smooth stems, white latex sap, and later pink flower clusters. Use only with strong confidence in identification and preparation.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-heavy edible species.
records.culinary_uses: Very young shoots can be boiled in changes of water and eaten as a cooked green by people familiar with proper preparation; this is a preparation-dependent plant, not a casual raw nibble.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, but this is a plant that needs careful handling and preparation knowledge.
records.edibility_detail: Not edible raw. Traditional food use requires correct species identification and proper boiling/cooking.

records.notes: Large coarse plant with huge compound leaves, thick hollow stems, and broad white umbrella-shaped flower clusters. Sap can irritate sun-exposed skin.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-heavy edible species.
records.culinary_uses: Very young peeled shoots and leaf stalks have been cooked traditionally, but this plant demands careful identification and skin protection.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional uses exist, but food use should be cautious due to look-alikes and sap sensitivity.
records.edibility_detail: Use only very young peeled shoots with solid identification and caution around sap exposure.

records.notes: This single plant entry replaces separate part listings: Dandelion greens, Dandelion flowers. Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Photos should focus on the whole plant for identification, while the text explains which part is used.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Dandelion greens: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses:
Dandelion greens: The plant to recognize is dandelion. The greens are one of the main edible parts.

Dandelion flowers: The plant to recognize is dandelion. The flowers are one of the usable parts.

records.notes: Orange-flowered ornamental escape with long strap-like leaves arising in fans.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary ornamental escape.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots, flower buds, and some other parts are edible in established daylily traditions.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with known culinary use, though some people do not tolerate it well.

records.notes: Trailing or low bramble, very similar to blackberry, with aggregate fruits and prickly canes.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry entry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are eaten fresh or used like blackberries in jam, syrup, and desserts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. This is best treated as a practical berry bucket near blackberry.

records.notes: Graceful conifer with short flat needles and tiny petioled attachment, unlike spruce. This entry is about the tree first and the tips second.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional evergreen tea for seasonal and respiratory support.
records.culinary_uses: Fresh spring tips make a bright evergreen tea or infusion and can be used in syrup or flavoring.
records.edibility_detail: Tips and tea are the practical use. Positive ID matters so you do not confuse conifers casually.

records.notes: Shrub with opposite leaves, flat white flower clusters, and drooping clusters of dark berries. This combined entry covers both flowers and berries.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional cold-season and immune-support plant, especially through cooked berry preparations and flower infusions.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers are used for fritters, cordial, and tea; ripe berries are cooked into syrup, jelly, wine, and preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Flowers are broadly used; berries should be fully ripe and usually cooked. Unripe fruit and non-fruit parts are not beginner food.

records.notes: Biennial with basal rosette first year and tall flowering spike with yellow flowers the second.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Root has traditional edible use, and young leaves or shoots may also be used cautiously in small amounts.
records.medicinal_uses: Well known from medicinal contexts, though here it is being tracked mainly for edible completeness.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with preparation; more of a broad-use species than a top-tier foraging food.

records.notes: Smooth green fiddleheads with a deep U-shaped groove on the inside of the stalk. Found in moist rich bottoms and river edges.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food plant rather than a medicinal one.
records.culinary_uses: Classic spring fiddlehead, best thoroughly cooked. Flavor is often compared to green beans or asparagus.
records.edibility_detail: Cook thoroughly before eating. Do not eat raw fiddleheads.

records.notes: Tall plant of burns, clearings, roadsides, and sunny edges. Leaves alternate, narrow, and pointed. Showy pink flowers in summer.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots are eaten like asparagus and leaves may be used in tea; young spring shoots can be peeled lightly and cooked while still tender.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing tea herb used for mild digestive and inflammatory complaints.
records.edibility_detail: Best when young. Older stems get tough quickly.

records.notes: Robust grapevine with larger leaves and strongly flavored fruits.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit vine entry.
records.culinary_uses: Strong-flavored grapes are good for juice, jelly, wine, and cooked fruit uses.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe grapes; many people prefer them processed.

records.notes: Biennial invasive with garlic scent when crushed, scalloped leaves, and white four-petaled flowers.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary here.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves, flowers, and seeds are used as garlicky mustard-flavored greens and seasonings.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and often worth using because it is invasive.

records.notes: Tall yellow composite of meadows, roadsides, and openings. The app keeps this as a broad goldenrod entry focused on tea and medicinal use.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional urinary-support and seasonal tea herb.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers and leaves are used mainly for tea rather than as a bulk food; the flavor is aromatic and slightly bitter.
records.edibility_detail: Mostly a tea and herb plant, not a calorie food. Correct ID matters because it is a broad genus entry.

records.notes: Spiny or prickly Ribes shrubs with translucent to colored berries and lobed leaves.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit entry.
records.culinary_uses: Tart berries are excellent in jam, pie, sauce, and preserves; some can be eaten fresh when fully ripe.
records.edibility_detail: Edible berries, usually best ripe and often best processed.

records.display_name: Goosefoot | close cousin of lamb's quarters
records.common_name: Goosefoot | close cousin of lamb's quarters
records.notes: Broad goosefoot-group compatibility entry kept because the old app treated some of these as close practical relatives rather than hard species lines.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food weed bucket.
records.culinary_uses: Used much like lamb’s quarters as a cooked green when young and tender.
records.edibility_detail: Treat like the lamb’s quarters lane: young greens are the main use.

records.notes: Lantern-husk fruits on a nightshade-family plant. Only ripe fruit in papery husks is the practical food use.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit entry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe husked fruits are sweet-tart and used fresh, in jam, pies, salsa, and drying.
records.edibility_detail: Eat only fully ripe fruit; green fruit and many other plant parts are not the same story.

records.notes: Twining legume vine with pinnate leaves, maroon-brown pea flowers, and edible tubers along the roots.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a food vine here.
records.culinary_uses: Protein-rich tubers are cooked like beans or potatoes and have major traditional food value.
records.edibility_detail: Edible tubers with real food value, though harvest takes patience.

records.notes: This entry is shown as the whole plant (Hawthorn) while the details describe the usable plant part. Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Photos should focus on the whole plant for identification, while the text explains which part is used.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Hawthorn berries: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Hawthorn berries: The plant to recognize is hawthorn. The berries are the edible part.

records.notes: This copyback combines the old app material for American hazelnut and beaked hazelnut: rounded leaves and edible nuts, with beaked or leafy husks depending on species.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food nut shrub.
records.culinary_uses: Nuts are edible raw or roasted and can be ground into meal.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe nuts. Usually gathered before squirrels clean you out.

records.notes: Opposite leaves and red hanging berry clusters on a wetland-edge shrub with white flower heads.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional shrub with food and medicinal associations.
records.culinary_uses: Bright red fruits are usually cooked into jelly, sauce, syrup, or blended preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but often best after frost or cooking. Raw flavor varies a lot.

records.notes: Kept as a broad honeysuckle bucket focused on flowers, not berries. Many honeysuckle fruits are not casual food.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional flower use exists in some systems.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers are sometimes sucked for nectar or used lightly in tea, but this is not a broad fruit-eating entry.
records.edibility_detail: Think flowers/nectar, not berries. This is a cautionary broad-genus entry.

records.notes: Rough climbing bine with opposite to paired leaves and cone-like female flowers.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional sedative and calming herb, especially via cones.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots can be eaten as a spring vegetable and cones are used for bitter aromatic flavoring.
records.edibility_detail: Shoots are the approachable edible use; cone use is more aromatic/herbal than snack food.

records.notes: Northern berry bucket for huckleberry-type fruits; often close in use to blueberries but not identical in seed feel and plant habit.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry entry.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet dark berries are eaten fresh or used like blueberries in desserts and preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.

records.notes: Tall sunflower relative with rough leaves, yellow flowers, and edible underground tubers.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food tuber.
records.culinary_uses: Tubers are eaten raw or cooked and have a crisp, sweet, nutty quality.
records.edibility_detail: Edible tubers, often best harvested after frost or in cool seasons.

records.notes: Added as a name-first plant entry for later detail expansion. Includes edible-flower and traditional topical-use interest in a single plant entry.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Edible-flower plant entry. Detailed culinary notes still to be added.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional topical medicinal context expected; detailed notes still to be added.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with white spring flowers and blueberry-like fruits ripening to purple.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food fruit in this app.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet berries are eaten fresh or used in pies, jams, syrups, and dried fruit mixes.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully colored and sweet.

records.notes: Low shrub or small conifer with sharp needle-like leaves and blue berry-like cones.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Berry-like cones are used sparingly as seasoning, tea, or flavoring rather than bulk food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic and urinary herb used in teas and preparations.
records.edibility_detail: Used in small amounts as flavoring or tea. Not a bulk fruit.

records.notes: Bog shrub with leathery alternate leaves and rusty underside, plus clusters of white flowers.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-aware northern medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves are used as tea in traditional northern practice rather than as a vegetable food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea plant with aromatic and medicinal history.
records.edibility_detail: Tea use is traditional, but this is a use-with-knowledge plant rather than a carefree daily beverage.
look_alikes: Bog rosemary and other bog shrubs

records.notes: Variable weedy plant with often whitish-mealy new growth and triangular to goosefoot-shaped leaves.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food weed here.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves are cooked like spinach and seeds are also edible with processing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young leaves and shoots, especially cooked.

records.notes: Low shrub of barrens, dry woods, and openings with sweet blue berries.
records.short_reason: Split from the generic Blueberries bucket into a real Upper Michigan species entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry and one of the best wild berries for baking, jam, syrup, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. One of the best practical wild blueberry species.

records.notes: Low northern berry bucket for tart cranberry-like fruits of bog and barrens habitats.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly fruit use in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Tart red berries used for sauce, jam, syrup, and mixed preserves rather than casual handful eating.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, usually best cooked or sweetened.

records.notes: This consolidated entry is about recognizing the tree and understanding sap season. Sugar maple is the best-known source, but the bucket is kept under maple in the new app.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food tree in this guide, though sap has traditional strengthening and spring-tonic associations.
records.culinary_uses: Sap is tapped in late winter and early spring for drinking fresh, concentrating into syrup, or boiling into sugar.
records.edibility_detail: Sap is the practical use. Tree identification matters before tapping, and yield varies by species and weather.

records.notes: Compatibility bucket for the winter-purslane/miner’s-lettuce lane kept from the older app structure.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a spring salad green entry.
records.culinary_uses: Tender juicy greens used fresh in salad or lightly cooked.
records.scientific_name: Claytonia perfoliata / Montia perfoliata style bucket
records.edibility_detail: Edible tender greens; the practical point is mild spring salad use.

records.notes: Tree with blackberry-like aggregate fruits borne singly or in loose groups.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a fruit tree entry.
records.culinary_uses: Soft sweet berries are eaten fresh or used in pies, jam, syrup, drying, and wine.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but very perishable and messy.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with opposite leaves, flat-topped white flower clusters, and dangling dark fruits that sweeten after frost or full ripening.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Blue-black ripe fruits can be eaten fresh when soft or used in preserves, leather, and cooked fruit preparations.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional shrub with some medicinal history, though primarily a food fruit here.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully dark and soft.

records.notes: Opposite pointed leaves with stinging hairs on rich disturbed ground, edges, and moist places. Harvest with gloves and positive identification.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tonic plant with urinary, nutritive, and general restorative use context; seeds also have small-quantity tonic use.
records.culinary_uses: Young tops are a serious spring green once cooked, blanched, dried, or otherwise processed; seeds can also be dried and used in small amounts in herb blends or foods.
records.edibility_detail: Must be cooked, blanched, dried, or otherwise processed before eating. Raw plants sting skin and can irritate the mouth.

records.notes: This entry is shown as the whole plant (Oak) while the details describe the usable plant part. Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Photos should focus on the whole plant for identification, while the text explains which part is used.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Acorns (leached): Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Acorns (leached): The plant to recognize is oak. The acorns can be leached to remove bitterness and make them more usable for flour, meal, or other foraged food uses.

records.notes: Shrubby true blueberry with oval leaves and soft-blue fruit.
records.short_reason: Split from the generic Blueberries bucket into a real Upper Michigan species entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry species here.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet ripe berries are good fresh, baked, or preserved.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.

records.notes: Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Original app included edible, culinary, or practical foraging use notes for this plant entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.

records.notes: Species-level cherry entry for pin cherry retained on the existing slug for compatibility with the current app structure.
records.short_reason: Species-level pin cherry record retained without changing slug wiring.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Original app included edible, culinary, or practical foraging use notes for this plant entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.

records.notes: Consolidated pine entry replacing separate needles/resin records. The tree must be recognized first; safe use depends on knowing which pine you have.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Needle tea is a traditional winter aromatic for respiratory and nutritive support; resin has old topical and chewing uses.
records.culinary_uses: Needles are used for tea, some species provide edible nuts, and resin has traditional chew, pitch, or flavor uses.
records.edibility_detail: Needle tea is the most approachable use. Pine nuts depend heavily on species, and resin use is more traditional than routine.

records.culinary_uses: Steeped as a vitamin C-rich tea
records.medicinal_uses: Immune support; respiratory relief

records.culinary_uses: Sap boiled into syrup or crystallized; resin and tips used in teas or chewed
records.medicinal_uses: Natural diuretic and tonic; antiseptic and mineral-rich

records.notes: Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Original app included edible, culinary, or practical foraging use notes for this plant entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.

records.notes: Low sprawling succulent with reddish stems and fleshy leaves, common in disturbed sunny ground.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Some traditional use, but mainly a food green.
records.culinary_uses: Succulent stems and leaves are used raw or cooked in salads, sautés, and pickles.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and crisp, especially when young and fresh.

records.notes: This single plant entry replaces separate part listings: Wild Leek (Ramp), Ramps (Wild onion). Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Photos should focus on the whole plant for identification, while the text explains which part is used.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Wild Leek (Ramp): Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Wild Leek (Ramp): The plant to recognize is ramps. The leaves and bulbs are the edible parts people usually seek.

records.notes: Cane-forming bramble with aggregate fruits that separate from the core when picked.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a food berry, though leaf tea traditions exist in some contexts.
records.culinary_uses: Classic fresh berry also used in jam, syrup, desserts, drying, and fruit leather.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. This broad entry stands in for the general raspberry bucket.

records.notes: Clustered translucent red berries on a currant shrub with lobed leaves.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit shrub entry.
records.culinary_uses: Bright tart berries are excellent for jelly, syrup, preserves, and baking.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries, though many people prefer them sweetened or processed.

records.notes: Small sour dock with narrow arrow-like leaves, common in poor soils and open disturbed places.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor medicinal history; mostly an edible tart green here.
records.culinary_uses: Tangy leaves used raw or cooked in small amounts for salads, soups, and sauces.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in small amounts; tartness is the point.

records.notes: Wild river-edge grape with climbing habit and clustered dark fruits.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a fruit vine entry.
records.culinary_uses: Small tart grapes used in jelly, juice, wine, and cooked preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, usually best processed.

records.notes: Small tree with mitten, unlobed, and three-lobed leaves on the same plant. This entry is tree-first and rolls the old part-specific record into the main sassafras entry.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive and tonic plant, centered especially on root bark in older use.
records.culinary_uses: Root bark is the classic flavoring for old root-beer style drinks, and leaves can be dried for filé-style seasoning.
records.edibility_detail: Root bark is the old classic use, but this is a flavoring/tea plant more than a bulk food.

records.culinary_uses: Used to flavor root beer or as filé powder
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional blood purifier; digestive tonic

records.notes: This single plant entry replaces separate part listings: Spruce gum, Spruce tips. Recreated locally from the original app baseline checklist for the modular standalone build. Photos should focus on the whole plant for identification, while the text explains which part is used.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Spruce gum: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses:
Spruce gum: The plant to recognize is spruce. The resin or gum is the useful part discussed in this entry.

Spruce tips: The plant to recognize is spruce. The young spruce tips can be used for a healthy and nutritious tea, syrup, or other bright evergreen uses.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with velvet twigs, alternate compound leaves, and dense upright red fruit clusters. Avoid white-berried poison sumac.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional astringent uses exist, though this is mostly a drink ingredient in the old app material.
records.culinary_uses: Fruit clusters are soaked in cool water to make tart drinks and can also flavor syrups and jellies.
records.edibility_detail: Edible fruit clusters, usually used as a souring ingredient rather than eaten whole.

records.notes: Woodland carrot-family plant with ferny leaves and sweet licorice-like scent.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic herb.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves, seeds, and roots can be used as sweet aromatic seasoning or tea ingredient.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but carrot-family caution applies: identification should be solid.

records.notes: Wetland iris-like plant with aromatic rhizome and a side-emerging spadix. This is a caution species and not casual beginner food.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-aware medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Rhizome has traditional candying, tea, and flavoring use in very small quantities by people who know the plant well.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive and aromatic medicinal plant.
records.edibility_detail: Use is traditional and knowledge-dependent. Treat this as a caution entry, not a carefree nibble plant.

records.notes: Low shrub with narrow, deeply lobed fragrant leaves that resemble a fern but is not a fern.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Aromatic leaves are mainly used for tea, seasoning, or infusion rather than as a vegetable.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic plant used in teas and simple preparations.
records.edibility_detail: Used mainly as tea or flavoring.

records.notes: Soft-leaved bramble with very large maple-like leaves and broad flattened red fruits.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry entry.
records.culinary_uses: Soft red berries are sweet and excellent but fragile, often best eaten quickly or turned into jam.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but delicate and not very shelf-stable.

records.notes: Added as a name-first plant entry for later detail expansion. Spring woodland edible.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Spring edible woodland plant entry. Detailed culinary notes still to be added.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use context may apply; detailed notes still to be added.

records.notes: Low blueberry with softly hairy stems/leaves and sweet blue fruits.
records.short_reason: Split from the generic Blueberries bucket into a real Upper Michigan species entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry species.
records.culinary_uses: Good fresh berry and preserve berry, often on smaller low plants.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.

records.notes: Low woodland and lawn flowers with heart-shaped leaves and purple, blue, yellow, or white blooms depending on species.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing herb.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and flowers are used in salads, teas, candies, and mild cooked greens.
records.edibility_detail: Widely edible leaves and flowers, especially young leaves.

records.notes: Bramble with thorny canes and aggregate fruits that keep the core when picked, unlike raspberries.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh or cooked berry used in jam, syrup, cobbler, baking, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Harvest dark glossy berries at full ripeness.

records.notes: Shrubby to small-tree plums with spring white blossom and yellow to red fruits depending on species and strain.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit-tree entry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are eaten fresh or made into jelly, butter, preserves, and desserts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit; tart skins and clingstone pits are common.

records.notes: Shrubby rose with prickly stems, compound leaves, pink flowers, and red to orange hips in late season.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Fruits can be used for tea, syrup, jelly, and puree once the irritating inner hairs and seeds are handled carefully.
records.medicinal_uses: Vitamin-C-rich traditional winter tea and tonic fruit.

records.notes: Tree or shrub of wet ground and waterways with narrow leaves on many species. This is a medicinal-first entry in the new app.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-heavy medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Not a primary food plant; practical use centers more on medicinal bark and occasional emergency sap/cambium traditions than on casual eating.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic bark medicine associated with pain, fever, and inflammation traditions.
records.edibility_detail: Not a routine food entry. Bark use belongs in the medicine lane and carries medication-style cautions.

records.notes: Low evergreen woodland plant with glossy leaves, white urn flowers, and red berry-like fruits.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea and aromatic medicinal plant.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and berries are used in tea, candying, and flavoring rather than as bulk food.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in modest use as tea, flavoring, or small fruit.

records.notes: Low shamrock-like plant with trifoliate leaves and small delicate flowers.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a flavoring green here.
records.culinary_uses: Tangy leaves and flowers are used fresh in small amounts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in modest amounts. Its bright tartness is the point, not bulk eating.
